event: Arab and Austronesian (Malay/Indonesian) navigators are believed to have sighted the uninhabited island in earlier centuries, but it had no permanent human population before European contact.
Portuguese navigators are credited with the first recorded European sighting of the island, which appears on early charts of the Mascarene group.
Portuguese explorer Pedro de Mascarenhas visits the islands, lending his name to the Mascarene archipelago that includes the future Réunion.
Dutch sailors visit the still-uninhabited island, which they referred to among the Mascarenes during their voyages in the Indian Ocean.
French sailors land on the island and France begins asserting a claim to the territory in the western Indian Ocean.
France formally takes possession of the island, then known as Mascarin, under the authority of the French East India Company.
A group of French mutineers from Madagascar is exiled to the island, marking one of the first attempts at human settlement.
The island is renamed Île Bourbon in honour of the French royal House of Bourbon.
The French East India Company sends the first organized settlers, establishing a permanent French colony on Île Bourbon.
Coffee cultivation is introduced to the island, becoming the foundation of a plantation economy worked increasingly by enslaved labour.
Mahé de La Bourdonnais becomes governor of the Mascarene islands, developing infrastructure and tying Bourbon's economy to neighbouring Île de France (Mauritius).
Control of the island passes from the bankrupt French East India Company to the French crown.
The island is placed under direct royal administration as a French crown colony.
Following the French Revolution, the island is renamed Réunion to commemorate the union of revolutionaries from Marseille and the National Guard.
The island is briefly renamed Île Bonaparte in honour of Napoleon Bonaparte.
British forces capture the island during the Napoleonic Wars, beginning a short period of British occupation.
The Treaty of Paris returns the island to France, which restores the name Île Bourbon while keeping nearby Mauritius under British rule.
Vanilla cultivation is established on the island, later boosted by techniques that make it a major export crop.
Edmond Albius, an enslaved boy on Réunion, invents a method for hand-pollinating vanilla orchids, revolutionizing the global vanilla industry.
France abolishes slavery in its colonies, freeing roughly 60,000 enslaved people on the island, which permanently readopts the name Réunion.
To replace freed slaves, planters begin large-scale recruitment of indentured labourers, mainly from India, transforming the island's population and culture.
Sugarcane becomes the dominant export crop, cementing the plantation economy as the island's economic backbone.
The opening of the Suez Canal diverts maritime traffic away from the Cape route, undermining Réunion's strategic importance and contributing to economic decline.
A railway line is opened along the coast, connecting the main towns of the island.
Réunion contributes soldiers and resources to France during the First World War.
Réunion is drawn into the Second World War as a French Indian Ocean territory.
Free French forces take control of Réunion from the Vichy administration after a warship arrives to rally the island to the Allied cause.
Réunion becomes an overseas department (département d'outre-mer) of France, granting its residents full French citizenship and integrating it into the Republic.
The French franc replaces the local colonial currency, integrating Réunion further into the metropolitan economy.
The Communist Party of Réunion is founded, becoming a leading voice for greater autonomy and social reform.
A controversial law allows the forced or incentivized relocation of thousands of Réunionese children to mainland France, a policy later condemned as a state scandal.
Réunion's local administration is reorganized as the island gains the status of an administrative region within France.
Roland Garros Airport near Saint-Denis expands as Réunion's main international gateway, improving links to mainland France.
France's decentralization laws give Réunion an elected regional council, increasing local self-government.
Riots erupt in the Chaudron district of Saint-Denis over unemployment and social inequality, highlighting deep economic tensions.
Réunion celebrates the 150th anniversary of the abolition of slavery, reinforcing 20 December (Fête Kaf) as a key cultural commemoration.
The euro replaces the French franc as Réunion's currency, fully aligning it with the Eurozone.
A massive chikungunya epidemic spread by mosquitoes infects roughly a third of the population, prompting a major public health response.
Réunion becomes an outermost region of the European Union with specific support measures under EU treaties.
The Pitons, cirques and remparts of Réunion National Park are inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site for their dramatic volcanic landscapes.
Debris from missing Malaysia Airlines Flight MH370 washes ashore on Réunion, drawing global attention to the island.
Réunion experiences weeks of 'gilets jaunes' (yellow vest) protests and roadblocks over the cost of living, echoing unrest across France.
Réunion endures successive waves of the COVID-19 pandemic, with health authorities managing outbreaks and vaccination campaigns across the island.
